bio.bio_blurb: Third pastor of the First Church of Hartford

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded Rev. Stone at Hartford — Hooker's pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: He succeeded Rev. Samuel Stone as pastor of the First Church in Hartford — the same pulpit Thomas Hooker had occupied until his death in 1647, and which Stone held after him. Joseph Haynes held the office until his own death in 1679. Harvard class of 1658; son of Gov.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Sarah married Yale's co-founder
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Haynes married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. in 1694. Pierpont secured the Yale charter in 1701. Sarah died October 27, 1696, the same day their daughter Abigail was born.

career.notes: Third pastor, succeeding Rev. Samuel Stone in Hooker's former pulpit.

bio.bio_blurb: Third pastor of the First Church of Hartford

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded Rev. Stone at Hartford — Hooker's pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: He succeeded Rev. Samuel Stone as pastor of the First Church in Hartford — the same pulpit Thomas Hooker had occupied until his death in 1647, and which Stone held after him. Joseph Haynes held the office until his own death in 1679. Harvard class of 1658; son of Gov.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Sarah married Yale's co-founder
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Haynes married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. in 1694. Pierpont secured the Yale charter in 1701. Sarah died October 27, 1696, the same day their daughter Abigail was born.

career.notes: Third pastor, succeeding Rev. Samuel Stone in Hooker's former pulpit.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Thomas Lord, one of the first settlers of Hartford, who came with the Hooker migration 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather walked to Hartford with Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather Thomas Lord was one of the original settlers of Hartford, coming with the Hooker migration from Cambridge in 1636. Her father Capt. Richard Lord was his son. Her daughter Sarah Haynes married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. in 1694 — the Yale co-founder.

narrative_blocks.header: She married Hartford's pastor, the governor's son
narrative_blocks.body: In 1668 Sarah Lord married Rev. Joseph Haynes, pastor of Hartford and son of Gov. John Haynes. Their daughter Sarah married Rev. James Pierpont of New Haven.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Gov. John Haynes; Harvard 1658; Hartford pastor.
parents.mother_research_notes: Granddaughter of Thomas Lord, original Hartford settler.

death.cause: died in childbirth — same day Abigail was born

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Connecticut's first governor
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Haynes was the daughter of Rev. Joseph Haynes, pastor of Hartford, and granddaughter of Gov. John Haynes. She married Rev. James Pierpont of New Haven in 1691.

narrative_blocks.header: She died soon after childbirth, at twenty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died on 7 October 1696, weeks after bearing her only child, Abigail. Abigail Pierpont later married Rev. Joseph Noyes of New Haven.

cross_connections.display_label: Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

narrative_blocks.header: She married her father’s successor
narrative_blocks.body: Orphaned of her mother at five weeks and raised by her stepmother Mary Hooker, Abigail married Rev. Joseph Noyes — the man who took her late father’s pulpit at New Haven’s First Church.

burial.plot_notes: Table stone, Ancient Burying Ground. Name on Founders Monument. Tombstone erroneously gives Mar 1, 1654; Winthrop letter Jan 9, 1654 confirms recent death.

cross_connections.display_label: was his Griffin shipmate and Fundamental Orders co-author
his granddaughter; she married Yale's founder James Pierpont
bore the Haynes name and married into the Hooker line
governed both New Haven and Connecticut colonies

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed with Thomas Hooker — became first governor
narrative_blocks.body: He and Rev. Thomas Hooker were close friends in England. Both emigrated in 1633 aboard the Griffin, settling at Newtowne (Cambridge). When Hooker led the migration to Connecticut in 1636, Haynes followed — becoming the first governor of the new Connecticut Colony in 1639.

narrative_blocks.header: First governor — eight terms — never back-to-back
narrative_blocks.body: He served as first Governor of Connecticut in 1639, alternating as governor and deputy governor eight separate terms until his death. Co-drafter of the Fundamental Orders (called the first written constitution, 1638/9). Invested £7,000-8,000 of his English fortune in Connecticut "to the ruine of his famylye in Englande."

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Sarah married Yale's co-founder
narrative_blocks.body: His son Joseph became pastor of Hartford's First Church. Joseph's daughter Sarah Haynes married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. in 1694 — who secured the Yale charter in 1701. Sarah died October 27, 1696, the same day their daughter Abigail was born.

narrative_blocks.header: He governed Massachusetts before following Hooker west
narrative_blocks.body: Haynes served a year as governor of Massachusetts Bay in 1635 before leaving for the Connecticut River. He presided over the banishment of Roger Williams, a judgment he is said to have later regretted.

narrative_blocks.header: He spent an English fortune building Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Haynes sank £7,000 to £8,000 of his English estate into the colony — by his son's account, "to the ruine of his famylye in Englande." He helped form the New England Confederation in 1643.

death.cause: epidemic — died summer 1655, same as second husband Samuel Eaton

parents.father_research_notes: Of Staple Inn, London and Earls Colne, Essex. 1631 will mentions Mabel.

narrative_blocks.header: She sailed on the Defense with her brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Harlakenden crossed to Massachusetts aboard the ship Defense in July 1635, with her brother Lt. Col. Roger Harlakenden. A portrait of her had been painted in England just before the voyage.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed, she married Rev. Samuel Eaton
narrative_blocks.body: After Gov. John Haynes died, Mabel married Rev. Samuel Eaton in November 1654. She died the following June, in 1655.

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
